speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Thursday, June 29, 1995. The survivor is a Halina Balfour. The interviewer is Carol Stulberg. Los Angeles, California, United States of America. Interview is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg, S-T-U-L-B-E-R-G. I have the privilege of interviewing Mrs. Halina Balfour. June the 29, 1995, in Los Angeles, California, for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. This is tape one. The survivor is Halina Balfour. We're in Los Angeles, California. This is June 29, 1995. Mrs. Balfour, would you please tell us what your name was at birth, and could you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Halina, H-A-L-I-N-A Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lodz, Poland, on March 13, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Roma, or Roma, Konigsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Konigsberg was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Konigsberg, that was her maiden name, and my father's name was Edmund Horowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you say you came from a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I think that my mother still had some vestiges of, of religion-- of religion left, but not my father or I. I wasn't taught any religion anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: I realize that you were a very young child at the outset of the war. You were probably only about six years old--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --but can you tell us what your earliest childhood memories are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Connected with the war? Or before?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before the war. What your earliest childhood memories are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that-- I remember the houses-- the house we lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very, very large apartment in one of my grandmother's houses, one of her properties. I don't remember how many bedrooms it had, but at least 10. And we lived there with my parents, and my grandmother, and various maids and cooks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: First name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah, and her surn-- surname was Konigsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your mother's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother's mother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other extended family that lived close by? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother had four sisters and five brothers. And they all lived not very clo-- but, you know, some lived in Warsaw and Krakow and Radom. Where else? I guess one or two must, must have lived in Lodz, but I don't remember very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Jewish holidays? Family get-togethers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They m-- there must have been, I'm sure of that. But you know, I seem to have a sort of selective memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having any friends that you played with at a very early age?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, well, do you remember anything leading up to the war? That would cause you to remember bad feelings, or fear, or trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well only, only when the bombing started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's as far as I-- back as I can go, as far as the war is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure . Lodz was bombed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Having to, to, to run to the shelter, which was sort of-- it wasn't really shelter. It was a, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a room below the building. I think they used it for, for keeping wine. And everybody from-- because the two-- the, the two buildings, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were connected, number five and seven. I don't remember the name of the street, but I do remember the name of-- the numbers. And in the middle was a, a yard, a round, large yard. Everybody from the two buildings used to run to the sh-- to the shelter. And that's the first thing I remember about the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents acting differently? Or talking about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after that, once the Germans came in, everybody had to wear a yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember wearing yellow a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Yes. And I think that is when my parents started to get worried about what might happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the Germans came in to Lodz, and you were made to wear the yellow star, what happened to you and your family? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that the husband of one of my mother's sisters, Dora, was the first to be killed. He was-- unless I'm very much mistaken-- with the Polish Army. And Dora came to live with us, and with her son, Arthur. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, her son wasn't born yet. She was pregnant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was born right before we left Lodz, where they, they were about to close the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was probably the beginning of 1940, would you say? Because the ghetto was closed around the end of April of 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that must have been then. And, you know, I just have end to end. Dora's son was born just before that because I remember that we escaped to Warsaw on a bus. And the baby must have been six days old, or three days old. And he cried, and apparently it was forbidden, because, of course, it was an illegal escape. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember at that time whether I was-- I was scared yet. It was all just sort of very strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the bus ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely. It was very long. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nothing happened. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: And on this bus ride, were you with both of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But I think I was only with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you took this bus ride to escape from Lodz, and you arrived to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Mother had-- one of my mother's sisters lived in the Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about arriving in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to Aunt Vanya's house. There were other people there. I don't know who, perhaps her husband's family. Aunt Vanya had one daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where it gets a little-- where, where I get lost. I, I, I lose the thread, somehow, the memory thread because I can't remember how-- I know that there were sort of goings away and comings back. And suddenly we were in the ghetto of Bochnia, but how we got there, that's one, one part of, of my life that sort of disappeared completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your memories of, of Warsaw and being in Warsaw really are nonexistent. There is one very, very really funny memory. What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I woke up one morning, and I told my mother-- because my grandmother and my mother's younger brother had stayed behind in Lodz. And I said to my mother, you know grandmother and Uncle Klemens are coming on a wooden plane, and they're made of wood, too. You know, this is something I remember as thought it were today because I woke up with that memory of that dream. And the m-- the most peculiar thing was that my grandmother and my uncle arrived that day from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a premonition.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a premonition. I thank God I've never had any more premonitions in my life because they scare me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after leaving Warsaw somehow-- and you don't remember how-- you arrived in Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but that was way after. That must-- a year, two years after, must be maybe '43. And I've lost all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember about how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] No. It's a part of-- I don't know why-- but it's simply erased. It just isn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Bochnia, what do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was fairly important in, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, your father did join you eventually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Bochnia, you were together with both your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: With both my parents, with my grandmother, and I think one or two of my mother's sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember being in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember being in the Bochnia ghetto, yes. We were there for quite, quite a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really not too bad because we lived in houses. We didn't live in, in barracks or anything like that. And it wasn't y-- scary yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know when, when you're-- when you're very small, it's-- you get scared because something happens in a given moment. But it's not like the grownups, who lived scared. You play with children your own age who were there, and you mainly don't see Germans. And the only thing, the only restrictions are you're not allowed to go beyond this street or beyond the other . And then you don't go to school, of course. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then it started to getting more scary because there was the first-- I remember the word in German. I don't remember it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aussiedlung. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they took a certain number of people and sent them to another camp, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: They liquidated people from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or liquidated them, or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my parents sent me away to outside the ghetto to a non-Jewish house. But I was there for about two nights, and I didn't know what to do. Nobody came to, to look for me, so I walked back. And that was scary. I walked back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- before walking back to the ghetto, spending two nights in this Gentile home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't really in their home. It was in the-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: An attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an attic that belonged to them. They, of course-- you know, it was very dangerous for them. They knew I was there, but they didn't want to know that I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember being up there for two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of like a game. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, not really like a game. I was scared part-time, but part-time it was sort of exciting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been warned that you were supposed to be quiet? Or not go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew all those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know these people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole time you were there for two days, you never saw the people whose home you were hiding--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, somebody I assume brought me food. But I didn't see any of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about walking back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear. That, that was scary because the streets were full of Germans. And I knew I had to avoid them. I knew I could get caught. So it took me an awfully long time to get back, but I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the route you took? Was it through--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I remember-- I knew the way back. And I must have taken the route which would put me sort of as far away from, from-- because they walked in pairs. They sort of patrolled the street. They knew something was going on in the ghetto, obviously. And that's why it wasn't-- it was sort of complicated getting back. But when I got back to ghetto, the whole thing was over. I mean the, the Aussiedlung had finished, and I don't remember how many people were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Aktion that they had selecting people at the time was over when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you got into the ghetto when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There wasn't anyone, or there were just Jews on the-- on the gate. And they let me through. There were-- there must have been other odd things of this type because I know that in the-- in the place we lived in, I remember there was a cellar. And they used to put the children in the cellar whenever there was the possibility of, of that happening. We always knew it because, as I said, my father-- I know-- I don't know what he was, exactly, but he always knew what was going to happen. And then there came the, the very big Aussiedlung, and they killed an awful-- they shot practically half the people in, in Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a witness to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I wasn't there when they were shot, but I had a-- she wasn't really adopted. She was somebody-- she was-- a fr-- my mother's-- my mother's friend's daughter, and her mother was dead. And she came to live with us. And I don't even-- that's another thing that I-- you see, Carol, I erased, by myself, an awful lot of things. And I can't get them back, like names. Well, she was killed in that. That, that was very unpleasant, this, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] because you see, at a certain time, if you had two children, you had to choose. You could keep only one. And they made my mother choose, of course, between her and me. And I suppose for my mother there was no choice, but she must have felt like a murderer. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It wasn't the sort of thing that we were separated. We, we were on one side of the street, and the ones who were going to be shot were on the other. And they knew it. And there was this boy who offered one of the Germans a fountain pen to, to let him go, to let him live. It was a boy I knew very well. He was a friend of mine. Just-- those are just sort of little things I remember that I haven't been able to, to erase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Sorry, but some things-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And after this-- after this Aktion where they killed so many people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. After that, we were taken to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being taken to Plaszow? And how you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All this bit from the-- from the time when they shot them, and they were lying there in, in the sun to, to how and when I got to Plaszow-- all that is out. I don't know. I don't know anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Plaszow and arriving there, what it was like for you finally arriving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not arriving there, really. I remember Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Plaszow? Describe it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow was the first barracks, to begin with. No more houses, no more apartments, just barracks. And Appellplatz-- you know, the place where they called you to count you, to see whether no one's-- and the place they hang, hang people with the orchestra playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them hanging people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. They always played, you know-- they always played very appropriate songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, like when they hung somebody, they, they played a German so-- song called 'Komm Zurick,' which in French-- it's really a French song. It's called 'J'attendrai.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what that means?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? J'attendrai?

speaker: interviewer
text: The title-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll wait for you. Komm Zurick's, it's come back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they mainly-- well, they mainly hung people who tried to escape. An awful lot of people did at that time. It was still-- there was still a possibility that to be hidden in some Pole's house. This was a camp, but it wasn't that strict. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And mostly hiding because-- or being in a place where one wasn't visible. I was there with my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your parents at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not sure. My mother was there. No. See, that's, that's where it gets mixed up because we were taken from Plaszow to, to Schindler's to [? Meil-- ?] to the [GERMAN]. And I know that I was taken with both my parents, which means that they must have been with me in Plaszow, but I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about your experience at Plaszow? Did you have to work while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. What, what we had to do is not to show ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you manage to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are ways. You just become half-- halfway invisible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You're not supposed to-- especially, you're not supposed to be seen by the Germans. So, you do your best not to be. You know, that there are working places where you can-- where other people work, but they help you to-- there's somewhere a little room where you can stay with the other children. Or, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. You manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever stay in the barracks during the day, hide in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- yes, I did what-- I had to go to the Appellplatz. There was no way out of that. I had to be counted. Because otherwise there would be somebody missing. And there were about-- at that time, there were about 60-- between 60 and 90 children in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I don't remember, really. I remember that I, I was sick quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who took care of you while you were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a friend of my mother's. Giza Schein. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think my mother was taken to to the [GERMAN] before, and that I was left behind initially in, in Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, again, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somehow I got to the [GERMAN] at one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't-- you don't remember how you came to be with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not initially. I know my parents were there, and then I was with them for not too long a period of time. I think it was about six months. And then we were at-- my mother and I were sent back to Plaszow. And I'm not sure whether my father was already then sent to Gross-Rosen, or later. But he, he wa-- he wasn't with us in Plaszow afterwards because that was when the big Aussiedlung came, when they closed Plaszow down. Something did happen before. The-- you know that-- I assume you know by now that each camp had a Jewish sort of-- they had the German director, et cetera, and then they had Jewish director. And the Jewish director and his wife and their-- I think they were their assistants or whatever-- tried to escape, and they shot them. They got caught, obviously, and shot. I remember that we had to-- we spent the whole day. We had to-- their bodies were thrown there. And we had to-- the whole camp had to walk by and look at them, just, you know, to see what would happen to any one of us if we escaped, I assume. I guess, you know, it's, it's many days, and, and all that. And some days, an awful lot of things happened. And the other days, nothing happened at all. And then it has been 50, 50 years, and it's sort of tough to remember after 50 years. You remember the most vivid detail, details, but not the be every day things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having other friends in Plaszow, and being able to ever act like a child, or play with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I had-- while there were children in Plaszow, I did-- my cousins were there. And there were other children. Play? Well, you may call it play, but very peculiar games.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think mainly escapists games. No, I don't mean trying to escape. But escapism, mental escapism. Going-- sort of children dream things up anyway. Well we did a little more than that. We tried to, I guess, just disappear mentally from there as, as often as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the food was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, it wasn't too bad, or at least it was sufficient. Black bread, marmalade, potato soup. You-- we didn't starve, not in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never had any interaction with the Nazi guards or the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not the children, at least. I don't know about the grown-ups, but the children definitely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember seeing the director of the camp, Amon Goth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, thank God. I do remember the-- there were-- all of them walked around with big dogs, especially when there was something going on. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, I don't know whether Goth knew that there were children there, and simply accepted the fact that, for some time, there would be. But you better kept out of his way, of all the Germans' way.
